world_map.raw:
Center point: 38.4863° N, 28.0375° E (Sardis)
Reach radius/trade zone description: gold's reach spans the entire ancient world — from Nubian and Egyptian gold sources to Mesopotamian Ur, to Mycenaean Greece, to Lydia, and onward to Roman Spain
Key nodes: Sardis (38.4863° N, 28.0375° E); Temple of Artemis, Ephesus (37.9495° N, 27.3639° E); Ur, Mesopotamia (30.9625° N, 46.1088° E); Mycenae, Greece (37.7311° N, 22.7559° E); Las Médulas, Spain (42.4694° N, 6.7297° W)
Suggested map era: 4600 BCE – 546 BCE
Historical map source: Wikimedia Commons, Map of Lydia in ancient times; Central Lydia Archaeological Survey maps

quiz.q: Who introduced the first pure gold/silver bimetallic coinage?
What river near Sardis was the natural source of Lydian electrum?
Where was the oldest known coin hoard discovered?
quiz.a: The Temple of Artemis at Ephesus

quiz.q: What two-step process did Lydian refiners use to separate gold and silver?
What is the Karun Treasure, and what happened to it?
quiz.a: A hoard of looted Lydian gold artifacts acquired by the Met Museum, later returned to Turkey in 1993

decision_simulation:
Scenario: It's 546 BC. Croesus must decide whether to trust his refined gold/silver coinage system to fund his war against Persia's Cyrus the Great, or rely on traditional wealth-in-goods.
Options: (1) Fund the war using minted coinage; (2) Rely on stored bullion and goods without standardized coins; (3) Delay the campaign to build more monetary reserves
Historical outcome: Croesus went to war using his wealth and was ultimately defeated and captured by Cyrus — but the bimetallic coinage system he created outlived his kingdom and became the model for coined money across the ancient world
EXPLORE IT (CURRENTLY OPEN — highest priority)

understand.economic_impact:
One-line summary: Aurora represents gold's 2,600-year-plus role as the one thing almost every culture on Earth has independently agreed is valuable, even without a government backing it.
Three key economic shifts: (1) Refining electrum into pure, standardized gold and silver let Croesus create the first bimetallic coinage system that could be trusted across borders; (2) gold's role as a store of value meant it could hold wealth across generations, wars, and currency collapses; (3) gold became the ultimate benchmark against which paper currencies were later measured (the gold standard)
Modern parallel: gold today functions as the world's classic "safe haven" asset — the thing investors buy when they don't trust anything else, from stocks to currencies
understand.political_impact:
One-line summary: Because gold doesn't answer to any single government, it has repeatedly outlasted the empires, kings, and currencies that tried to control it.
Three key political shifts: (1) Gold reserves became a symbol and tool of state power, from Lydian treasuries to modern central bank vaults; (2) the 20th century's gold standard tied national currencies' credibility directly to gold reserves until it was abandoned; (3) repatriation fights over looted gold artifacts, like the Karun Treasure, became modern political and legal battles between nations
Modern parallel: central banks today still hold large gold reserves (like the Bank of England's vaults) as a hedge against political and financial instability, just as ancient kingdoms once did
understand.why_it_still_matters:
Plain-language answer: Gold has been valuable for so long, in so many different places, that people trust it almost automatically — even today, when scary things happen in the world, people still rush to buy gold because it has never once become worthless.
Connection to today's world: When you hear that gold prices are rising during a war or a financial crisis, that's the same instinct people had thousands of years ago at the Pactolus River — the belief that gold is one of the few things that will always be worth something.
